speaker: interviewer
text: This is Bill Basch. This is roll six, Bill Basch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I take you guys out to dinner? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're ready whenever. You were talking about the DP camp in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, in the DP camp in Italy, finally I discovered that someone told me that I have a brother who is alive. And it was a total bewilderment to me. After tracing and, and, and, and running around all over Italy, trying to find what camp he's in, I discovered the camp that he was in.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the night that I arrived, they tell me, [NON-ENGLISH] Basch? He left yesterday on a boat to Israel. They allowed 500 kids to go into Israel-- English allowed them-- and my brother got on the boat to help those kids. And he forgot to get off. So he winds up in Israel. He winds up in the Irgun. He winds up to fight for the liberation of Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six, seven years later, we decided that we'll all get together in this country because he didn't want to stay there. And this was the only place where-- through some circumstances I had to come first, because my sister couldn't get into Israel. Fate had it so that we all got together here, in this country. And after I arrived in this country, several years-- several weeks later, I met a young lady who was--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in 1947, November the 21st. And started working November the 22nd.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a cleaning store. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Shortening pants.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Los Angeles. Because I came to Los Angeles to the same uncle that has many years ago sent us an affidavit that we-- my father missed out on. Then my first goal was to learn the English language. And I have-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to night school. And I was introduced to a young lady who also was from Czechoslovakia who was a very sweet young lady. And the teacher thought that we would make a very fine couple. And two years later, we got married. And we were married for 47 years. We had three children. First we had a son, Martin, Daughter Esther, and a third child, Nanette.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us the years when they were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin was born in 1953. Esther was born in 1955. And Nanette was born in 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name was Rose Holly. Holly was her nickname, everybody called her Holly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she also a Holocaust survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was also a Holocaust survivor. And unfortunately, we will not get into a lengthy detail on this. It is a very sad story. But my wife has developed an illness which was called-- the professional language, thrombocytopenic purpura. After months and years of research and surgeries, 15 years of illness, 15 years of going to every available doctor, we could not discover the cause for it. Finally, my doctor, who was from-- one of the greatest hematologists from Salt Lake City, Dr. Wintrobe-- every doctor knows of him. He's the father of hematology-- has contacted the University of Oslo, where they did a lot of research on Holocaust survivors. And they came up with the conclusion that there's no cure. Because my wife's illness was really due to the poison that they gave her while she was a little girl in Auschwitz, and they didn't want her to have the period, so they stopped the period. Some people never had the reactions, other people later in life. Everyone had some reaction. That's called the syndrome, Concentration Camp Syndrome. In her case, it started deteriorating the interiors of her blood vessels. And she had several pulmonary embolisms. And eventually she died from cerebral hemorrhage, after 15 years being ill. But she gave me three beautiful children. And my daughter gave me gorgeous two little girls, which gave me-- give me enormous pleasure, Rhiann and Jaclyn. And my son gave me two boys, Maxi and Abel. However, I have another granddaughter that my son married-- lovely girl, Maria, who had a little girl, who is my granddaughter, equally to all my grandchildren. So she's my fifth grandchild. And that is the joy and the pleasure of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my life has been fulfilled. Economically, I have recovered, by starting being a contractor. Then I became a manufacturer in the fashion business. I started investing in the real estate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And God was with me, and God helped me. And I have been able to retire at age of 61. And I've lived in this place, really, since I've lost my wife. My wife and I lived in a very lovely home in Westwood. And I could not cope living in the same house, where so much pain and suffering existed. So I moved out of the house. And I'm leasing the house now, and I live here. But we get together, the family. We have great love and great joy. And we live a very traditional Jewish life. And Judaism is, is, is part of our life. And presently, I'm trying to, to repay to society some of it by going out to schools, and I lecture to school children. I lecture to them about the Holocaust. But not so much about the atrocities of the Holocaust, but why and how it happened. And I'm trying to explain to them that what hatred could do, what it did to a modern country, how it can destroy the self and then destroy a nation and destroy people. And-- it's amazing the reaction I get from kids. Sometimes I see tears on the little kids. They're, they're, they're so interested. And I have got a booklet of letters from these kids, some of them expressing the gratitude for showing them what hatred towards other people do, pledging that they will never hate anyone. And they would like to be in contact with me. Some write to me, and I write back to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the least I can contribute. I hope to do more. I hope to get involved with the Wiesenthal Center a little more, lecture more. Because the only thing I can do is possibly save some kids from becoming [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] haters instead of lovers of humanity.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, I, I realize time is going by. So I just would like to leave this message to my grandkids, that children, some of you I know-- my grandkids, my great-grandkids I don't know yet. But you will be around, you will come around, hopefully. I will be gone a long time by then. If anything I can teach you is the love for humanity, the love for God, is greater than any earthly pleasures, it is human beings that make the world good or bad. And you are part of-- you're a human being, part of a generation that can make the world anything you want it to be. It will be in your hands, not in mine, I'll be gone. I hope that my experience has taught you one thing-- do not hate. Remember, but do not hate. That is self-destructive. That will hurt you. I do not hate. I have not forgotten. And I hope you that you kids will somehow remember me in that frame.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very obvious you don't hate. Thank you for a very inspiring testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: That you for taking my story. I'm grateful to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Bill. We're going to look at pictures of your family now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. It would be very kind. This picture is of my mother and father. To best of my memory, my mother was at that time, about 35, 38 years old. My father was about four years older. And was-- Martin Basch is my father. Gizella Basch is my mother. And it was taken in front of our house in the small village of Sasovo.

speaker: interviewer
text: When? When was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: When it was taken? I would estimate it was taken in 1940. This is our home in Sasovo. This house was built in turn of the century by my grandfather, who later adopted my mother. The man's-- so that's where we were raised. This is what we remember. And that this, this home, which is so dear to all of us. And the the people standing-- the man standing alone is my father. That little boy is myself. And the boy next to him is my brother. The others are really guests. Or not so much guests as customers, because that was the entrance to the store that we had. It may not seem much. But that was the store that we had. And it serves the whole village.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this, this was taken probably also around 1940, '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your, your father's last name and the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's last name, of course, Martin Basch. And then it's Bill Basch, the little guy. Ted Basch, the next one. I don't recognize the other people. They were just people from the store that stood by there. This is, again, in front of our house. This picture was taken approximately 1941. And this consists of the whole Basch family. This is the last photo that we have ever been able to find of the whole family. And it consists on the following people. On the top line, on the left is Arthur, my oldest brother who, the best to my knowledge, was killed in Auschwitz. Then next to him is my mother, who was 44 years old. In 1944, on Yom Kippur, she was burned in Auschwitz. We know that. The next person to the right is my father. And we don't know when he died or where he died. The boy standing next to my father is my oldest brother-- my older brother, not my oldest-- my older brother, who survived, who is with me here. His name is Ted Basch. And he's in Los Angeles. And that little guy is myself, Bill Basch. And the two girls on the lower part is Edith, to the left, who survived. Edith Basch, or Tyler, her married name. She lives in Palm Springs. And the little girl was called Pearl. And as soon as the family entered the camp, she was the first one to be sent to the left, to the, to the gas chambers, because she had some food in her hands, and she refused to show what it was. And Eichmann at that time got furious at her and hit her with a stick and says, you're going to die today. And put her-- sent her to the, to the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that is-- the survivors are now the girl to the left, Edith, and the two boys to the right, Ted Basch and Bill Basch. Those are the three, the others are gone. And how do you know when your mother died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my sister was with my mother. My mother stopped eating, because she knew that both of them cannot make it. So she said you, at least, may survive. I will not. On Yom Kippur in 1944, the Germans wanted to prove to God that they are not scared of him. They killed more people on that day in Auschwitz than any other day. That was the day when they went through the barracks and grabbed my mother out of my sister's hands and took her to Auschwitz-- took her to the crematorium. So we know specifically the date. And that's why I have [NON-ENGLISH] on every Yom Kippur for my mother. This picture was taken approximately 1941. Carpathia, which was at that time Czechoslovakia or Hungary, now Ukraine. Nevertheless, the kids would get it up to shovel the snow and clear the road. And all of those kids are from 8 to 14. That was the first initiation to forced labor for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are all Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are all Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if any of them survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know of any one of them. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I am on right hand, with the right hand stretched out, on the right side of the picture. The right hand stretched out, that is me. This is my wife's engagement picture, made in Los Angeles in 1949. And at that time, she was 20 years old. This is what she gave me as an engagement gift. She is, of course, died--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Rose Holly Basch. And she died June 6-- March 6, 1978, at age of 49.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Branberger, Rose Holly Branberger.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Czechoslovakia also. These are the three that survived the Holocaust. To the left is my brother, Ted Basch, born in Czechoslovakia in 1925. To-- in the middle is my sister, Edith-- now Edith Tyler-- born 1928. And I am to the right. And I was born in 1927 in Czechoslovakia. These are the three that survived the Holocaust from seven, out of seven. This picture is the latest family picture, the only one that we have of the complete family. There I am in the center, one survivor who has-- God has blessed with a beautiful family. And I've recovered what God wanted me-- fine Jewish family. To the left of me is my daughter-in-law, Maria. And next to her, is my son-in-law, Mark Katzman, who is married to my daughter, who is next to Mark, to the left. Below them are the two little girls, Rhiann Katzman to the left. And to the right of her is Jaclyn Katzman. Now, starting with me again, to the right, next to me, is my granddaughter Heidi. She's the daughter of my son, who is to the right of Heidi. That's my son. And my daughter Nanette, my third child, is to the right of Martin. And the two little boys are the sons of Martin and Maria. And just as Heidi is the daughter that came along with the marriage who is also my loving, dear grandchild. That consists of the full family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This taken-- was taken at Heidi's bat mitzvah, just recently. Heidi was not born Jewish. But she has, by her choice-- because she still has a father-- by choice, has converted to Judaism. She has chosen on her own to be Jewish. And she has had a bat mitzvah, which was unbelievable. She has conducted the complete Musaf service, the complete Torah reading. And it was just unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: When? And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was done just about a week ago. A week ago, which is March 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Palos Verdes Estates. This is my son, Martin Basch and his lovely bride, Maria Galletti-Basch. They were married in San Pedro-- well, actually, in Los Angeles. But she is from San Pedro. And they were married on February 25, 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it can't be.

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin, forgive me. Big mistake. You were married in 1984, not '54. This is my beautiful daughter, Esther Gita Basch Katzman, married to Mark Katzman, who is my son-in-law. They were married on November 19, 1984 and produced two gorgeous little girls. Thank you, kids. This picture was taken at the beach, just a casual little snapshot with my $5 million or $5 billion in grandchildren. The one in the center, of course, is Papa, who loves his little ones. To the left is Maxi, Max Basch. Next to him is the little girl, is Katzman-- oh my God, I'm forgetting my grandchildrens' name-- [LAUGHS] Rhiann-- Jaclyn Katzman, the youngest one. Next to her, to the right is Rhiann Katzman. And then there's Abi Basch next to my left shoulder, and Heidi Basch, the oldest grandchild, next to Abi. And this is my future grandchildren and to you that I am dedicating this film. I hope you will cherish it and pass it on to your grandchildren, so they know where our people were, and what they suffered so that you can be Jews in the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Bill, when was this taken and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken at the beach of San Pedro, at a private beach, approximately 1982. About two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In San Pedro, California.
